step2_rag_chunks: Keeping a diary of triggers and cravings can help identify patterns and develop better coping strategies.

Therapist: That's a valid concern about past failures - many people who quit smoking have tried several times before succeeding. Have you noticed anything that was different about those times when you were able to resist the urge to smoke for a longer period, even if it was just for a day or two?
Patient: I remember when I was pregnant with my second child, I didn't smoke at all for 8 months. But after I had the baby, I just didn't think about it as much anymore, you know?
Therapist: Since you didn't smoke for 8 months during your pregnancy, I'm curious, was it the pregnancy itself or being a mother at that time that made you less interested in smoking?
Patient: I think it was being a mother, especially knowing that I had a baby inside me, it just made me want to be healthier for them.

step2_rag_chunks: Having even one person who knows you are trying to quit and supports you makes the process significantly easier. This is not about having someone watch over you â€” it is about having someone to call when the urge is strong, or someone who will not offer you a cigarette. Who in your life could be that person for you?
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.
Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.
